content.text: Automated pipelines that run on every code change — building, testing, and deploying software without manual intervention — eliminate the human error and inconsistency that accumulate when teams deploy services by hand. For agentic AI services, these pipelines carry special responsibilities: they must validate not only deterministic unit logic but also model-backed behaviour through evaluation suites, produce immutable container images traceable to exact git commits, and gate promotions through staged environments before traffic reaches end users.

Continuous Integration (CI) is the practice of merging every developer's work to a shared branch frequently — typically multiple times per day — and running an automated verification suite on each merge or pull request. The key insight is

: catching bugs at commit time costs far less than catching them in production. CI systems listen for push or pull-request events from version control, spin up a clean environment, install dependencies, and execute a series of quality gates: static analysis, type checking, unit tests, and integration tests.

Continuous Delivery (CD) extends CI by automating the steps that move a verified artifact — a Docker image, a compiled binary, a package — into one or more deployment environments. In its strictest form (Continuous Deployment), every green build goes to production automatically. More commonly, teams use Continuous Delivery with a lightweight approval gate between staging and production. The pipeline produces an immutable artifact tagged with the git commit SHA, then promotes that exact artifact through environments; it never rebuilds from source at deploy time.

For agent services, the pipeline extends further: it must run AI evaluation suites to check whether prompt or tool changes degrade model behaviour, because unit tests alone cannot verify emergent LLM reasoning. A typical agentic CI/CD pipeline therefore has an extra tier — a fast smoke eval on every merge to main, and a comprehensive golden eval running nightly — layered on top of the conventional code quality gates.

Manual deployments of AI services accumulate several failure modes that are easy to ignore on small teams and catastrophic at scale. A developer SSH-ing into a server to update code will use whatever Python environment is active, may forget to restart the service, and will produce no audit trail linking the live system to a specific commit. When that deployment breaks production, root-cause analysis becomes a detective exercise rather than a tag lookup.

For agentic AI, the stakes are higher than for conventional APIs. Prompt changes, new tool definitions, and updated retrieval configurations alter the behaviour of every request the agent processes. Unlike a code change that produces a compilation error, a prompt regression may be invisible to standard tests yet cause the agent to refuse valid requests, hallucinate tool arguments, or loop indefinitely. A CI/CD pipeline that enforces eval gates before promotion is the only scalable mechanism to catch these regressions before users encounter them.

Beyond correctness, CI/CD serves critical operational goals: traceability (every production deployment links to a git SHA and a pipeline run), reproducibility (staging always runs the same image that reaches production), and speed (automated pipelines can complete in four minutes what a careful manual deploy takes forty).
— lint, type checking, tests, and evals block broken code from merging, rather than relying on reviewer attention to catch every class of error.
— every Docker image is tagged with its git SHA; 'what is running in production' becomes a one-command lookup rather than tribal knowledge.
— staging and production run identical images built by identical steps, eliminating 'it worked on my machine' environment discrepancies.
— a PR author knows within minutes whether their change breaks anything, rather than discovering failures during a weekly release ceremony.
— because every version is a tagged immutable image, reverting to the last known-good state is a single command that redeploys a previous tag.
— pipeline logs link every production change to a pull request, reviewer, timestamp, and test results, satisfying compliance requirements without extra tooling.
— CD pipelines can route a fraction of traffic to the new version first, limiting blast radius if a regression slips through automated gates.
— YAML configuration files are code that must be kept current as dependencies, toolchains, and deployment targets evolve. Flaky tests or misconfigured jobs block legitimate merges.

— running a comprehensive LLM evaluation suite on every commit calls external model APIs and takes tens of minutes, adding API cost and slowing the feedback loop. Eval tiers must be designed carefully.

— CI jobs need API keys, registry credentials, and cloud permissions. Storing secrets safely (GitHub Encrypted Secrets, OIDC federation, Vault) adds setup complexity that teams often defer, creating security debt.
— a pipeline that runs only unit tests and skips agent-level evals creates a false signal: green CI does not guarantee the agent still behaves correctly.

— writing pipeline YAML, Dockerfiles, and eval harnesses takes days to weeks upfront. Smaller projects may ship faster initially without this infrastructure, then pay back the debt as complexity grows.
— failures in CI are sometimes environment-specific (runner OS, network conditions, flaky external services) and require understanding the CI platform's own abstractions to diagnose.
A CI/CD pipeline begins with an event: a

to a branch, the opening of a pull request, the merging of a PR to main, or a scheduled cron trigger. The CI platform (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, Jenkins) receives a webhook from the version control system, selects a pipeline definition (a YAML file committed to the repository), allocates a fresh runner VM or container, and begins executing jobs.

Jobs are isolated units of work that can run in parallel or in dependency order. Within each job, a series of named steps runs commands against the checked-out repository. Steps share a filesystem but jobs do not (unless artifacts are explicitly passed between them using upload/download actions). This isolation prevents environment pollution: each job starts from a clean state defined by its runner image.

Continuous Integration runs on every push or pull-request event. It must complete quickly — under ten minutes is a strong target — to keep the developer feedback loop tight. The standard stages for an agent service, in dependency order, are:
— restore a cached dependency lock-file install; fall back to a full install on cache miss. Using a lock file (
) ensures reproducible versions across runner, developer machine, and production image.
for Python linting and import ordering, and
) for type checking. These catch entire classes of bugs with zero runtime cost.

exercises pure logic with no external I/O. Well-factored agent code separates prompt construction, tool dispatch, and result parsing into testable pure functions that can be exercised without calling an LLM.
uses CI service containers (a Postgres or Redis sidecar) to test persistence, caching, and tool interactions without calling real external APIs. External APIs are mocked with recorded fixtures.
— a small subset of golden evaluation cases (10 to 50) that call the real model API to verify core agent behaviour. Protected by a CI secret; skipped on forks where secrets are unavailable.

CD triggers on merges to the main branch or on explicit version tags. It may be a separate workflow file or additional jobs that depend on the CI workflow passing via branch protection rules. The standard stages are:
using a multi-stage Dockerfile. The image is tagged with the exact commit SHA that passed CI.
— Trivy or Snyk scans the image for known CVEs in OS packages and Python dependencies. Failed scans with high or critical severity findings block promotion.
— the scanned image is pushed to a container registry (ECR, GCR, GHCR). The tag is the git SHA; the
tag is never used in production.
or an ECS task-definition update deploys the SHA-tagged image to a staging namespace with non-production data and model quotas.
— a broader eval suite (50 to 200 cases) runs against the live staging deployment. Results are compared to a stored baseline; a pass-rate drop beyond a threshold fails the pipeline.
— manual approval via GitHub Environments protection rules, or automated approval if all metrics are above threshold.
— 10% of production traffic routes to the new image. Error rate and latency metrics are monitored for several minutes. A failed canary triggers automatic rollback.
— after a soak period, traffic shifts 100% to the new image.

The diagram below shows the full CI/CD pipeline for an agentic AI service, from a code push through quality gates to canary production deployment. The CI phase (blue) runs on every pull request and every push. The CD phase (green) runs on merges to main. The amber gate is the approval checkpoint between staging and production. The purple nightly block runs independently on a cron schedule.

attrs.alt: CI/CD pipeline architecture for an agentic AI service, showing CI phase with lint, unit tests, integration tests, Docker build, and push to registry, followed by CD phase with staging deploy, smoke eval, approval gate, canary rollout, and full production deployment

content.text: Key structural choices in this architecture: CI and CD are separate workflow files sharing only the container image artifact. Evals are tiered — a fast smoke subset runs post-merge in CI, a broader suite runs post-staging, and a comprehensive nightly run accumulates historical pass-rate trends. The canary stage limits blast radius: only 10% of real users are exposed to a regression before automatic rollback fires.

Even a solo developer benefits from automated CI. A five-step pipeline (install, lint, type-check, test, build) running on every push catches mistakes that code review misses and enforces a discipline of keeping the test suite green. The setup cost is one hour; the payoff compounds every week as the codebase grows.
Add CD when deploying to shared environments

As soon as a staging or production environment is shared across more than one person, manual deployment coordination becomes error-prone. CD should be introduced when: the same service is deployed by multiple people; the deployment involves more than one component (API, worker, and database migration); or rollback needs to happen faster than a developer can log in and run commands manually.
When not to use fully automated production deployment

Fully automated production deployment — Continuous Deployment without an approval gate — is inappropriate for agent services that interact with real users, execute tool calls with side effects (sending emails, modifying databases), or process sensitive data. A manual approval gate costs seconds to click and prevents a prompt regression from immediately affecting all production traffic. Reserve fully automated production deployment for internal tooling or sandbox services where the blast radius of a regression is acceptable.

Not all evaluation cases should run in every pipeline stage. A fast PR check must complete in under ten minutes; a full golden evaluation may take two hours and cost several dollars in model API calls. The practical breakdown for a production agent service:
— unit tests and integration tests only. Zero LLM calls. Must complete in under 8 minutes.
— unit, integration, and smoke eval (10 to 50 cases, fast model variant, deterministic settings).
— 100 to 500 cases covering all agent capabilities and representative edge cases.
— full golden suite of 500+ cases; results stored for regression tracking and pass-rate dashboards.

The following walkthrough builds a complete GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline for an agent service. It assumes a Python project with a Dockerfile, using uv for dependency management, pytest for tests, and AWS ECR as the container registry.

.github/
  workflows/
    ci.yml          # runs on every PR and push
    cd.yml          # runs on merge to main
Dockerfile
pyproject.toml
uv.lock
src/
  agent/
    api.py
    tools.py
    prompts.py
tests/
  unit/
  integration/
  evals/
    smoke/          # 50 fast golden cases (called on merge to main)
    golden/         # 500 full cases (nightly only)

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  quality:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up uv
        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v4
        with:
          enable-cache: true
          cache-dependency-glob: "uv.lock"

      - name: Install deps
        run: uv sync --frozen

      - name: Lint
        run: uv run ruff check .

      - name: Type check
        run: uv run pyright src/

      - name: Unit tests
        run: uv run pytest tests/unit -q --tb=short

  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: quality
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v4
        with:
          enable-cache: true
          cache-dependency-glob: "uv.lock"
      - run: uv sync --frozen
      - name: Integration tests
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:test@localhost/test
        run: uv run pytest tests/integration -q --tb=short

  smoke-eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: integration
    # Run only on pushes to main; skip on fork PRs (no secrets)
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v4
        with:
          enable-cache: true
          cache-dependency-glob: "uv.lock"
      - run: uv sync --frozen
      - name: Smoke eval
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: uv run python tests/evals/run_eval.py

# .github/workflows/cd.yml
name: CD
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
  IMAGE: agent-api

permissions:
  id-token: write   # required for OIDC
  contents: read

jobs:
  build-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image-tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tag }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Configure AWS via OIDC
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/github-actions-ecr
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Log in to ECR
        uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v2

      - name: Set image tag
        id: meta
        run: |
          echo "tag=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE }}:${{ github.sha }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build image
        run: docker build -t ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tag }} .

      - name: Scan for CVEs
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tag }}
          format: table
          exit-code: 1
          severity: CRITICAL,HIGH

      - name: Push image
        run: docker push ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tag }}

  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: build-push
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v4
        with:
          enable-cache: true
          cache-dependency-glob: "uv.lock"
      - run: uv sync --frozen

      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent-api \
            api=${{ needs.build-push.outputs.image-tag }} -n staging
          kubectl rollout status deployment/agent-api -n staging --timeout=120s

      - name: Staging smoke eval
        env:
          AGENT_URL: https://staging.agent.example.com
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: uv run python tests/evals/run_eval.py

  deploy-prod:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: deploy-staging
    environment: production   # requires manual approval
    steps:
      - name: Canary deployment (10% pods)
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent-api-canary \
            api=${{ needs.build-push.outputs.image-tag }} -n production
          kubectl rollout status deployment/agent-api-canary \
            -n production --timeout=120s

      - name: Wait and verify canary metrics
        run: sleep 300   # 5-minute soak; in prod use a metric query

      - name: Full rollout
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent-api \
            api=${{ needs.build-push.outputs.image-tag }} -n production
          kubectl rollout status deployment/agent-api -n production
Example 1: minimal CI for a local agent project

This minimal pipeline runs on every push to any branch, executes lint and tests in a single job, and provides fast feedback without any cloud credentials. It is the right starting point for a new agent repository before CD infrastructure exists.

# .github/workflows/ci-minimal.yml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Python 3.11
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: pip

      - name: Install deps
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Lint
        run: ruff check .

      - name: Type check
        run: pyright src/

      - name: Unit tests
        run: pytest tests/unit -q --tb=short
Example 2: Python eval runner called from CI

A standalone evaluation harness that CI invokes directly. It loads golden test cases from a directory, calls the agent, compares outputs to expected values, and exits non-zero if the pass rate drops below a threshold — causing the pipeline to fail.

# tests/evals/run_eval.py
"""Eval harness for the agent service. Called from CI on merge to main."""
import json
import sys
import os
from pathlib import Path
import anthropic

PASS_THRESHOLD = float(os.environ.get("EVAL_THRESHOLD", "0.90"))
EVAL_MODEL = os.environ.get("EVAL_MODEL", "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022")


def load_cases(suite_path: Path) -> list[dict]:
    cases = []
    for f in sorted(suite_path.glob("*.json")):
        cases.append(json.loads(f.read_text()))
    return cases


def call_agent(client: anthropic.Anthropic, case: dict) -> str:
    """Call the model with the case's system prompt and input."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=EVAL_MODEL,
        max_tokens=512,
        temperature=0,          # deterministic for evals
        system=case["system_prompt"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip()


def evaluate_case(case: dict, output: str) -> bool:
    """Check output against expected result. Supports exact match or keyword list."""
    if case.get("exact_match"):
        return output == case["expected"]
    keywords = case.get("expected_keywords", [])
    return all(kw.lower() in output.lower() for kw in keywords)


def main() -> None:
    suite_dir = Path(os.environ.get("EVAL_SUITE", "tests/evals/smoke"))
    cases = load_cases(suite_dir)

    if not cases:
        print(f"No eval cases found in {suite_dir}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print(f"Running {len(cases)} eval cases from {suite_dir} (model: {EVAL_MODEL})")
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()   # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env
    passed = 0

    for case in cases:
        try:
            output = call_agent(client, case)
            ok = evaluate_case(case, output)
        except Exception as exc:
            print(f"  [ERROR] {case['id']}: {exc}")
            ok = False

        status = "PASS" if ok else "FAIL"
        print(f"  [{status}] {case['id']}: {case['input'][:60]!r}")
        if ok:
            passed += 1

    rate = passed / len(cases)
    print(f"\nResult: {passed}/{len(cases)} passed ({rate:.1%})")

    if rate < PASS_THRESHOLD:
        print(f"FAIL: pass rate {rate:.1%} < threshold {PASS_THRESHOLD:.0%}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print("OK: eval suite passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

A production customer-support agent service handling 50 000 daily conversations uses the following pipeline structure. On every pull request, a four-minute CI job runs ruff, pyright, 1 200 unit tests, and 80 integration tests against Postgres and Redis service containers. No LLM calls are made at this stage; tool-dispatch logic, prompt construction, and parsing functions are all exercised via unit tests with fixture responses captured from the real API.

On merge to main, CD builds a multi-stage Docker image (builder stage installs dependencies, runtime stage copies only the application), scans with Trivy (any critical CVE blocks the pipeline), and pushes to ECR tagged with the 40-character git SHA. A 60-case smoke eval then deploys the image to a staging Kubernetes namespace and calls the agent over HTTPS, comparing outputs against a baseline JSON file stored in S3. The pipeline uploads the full eval report as a GitHub Actions artifact.

The staging environment uses a separate Anthropic API key with a lower rate limit and a dedicated billing alert at $50 per day. After the smoke eval passes, a GitHub Environment protection rule requires one engineer to click Approve. Approval triggers a canary Kubernetes deployment — 10% of pods updated to the new image — while Datadog monitors error rate and p95 latency for five minutes. If either degrades by more than 20%, a

fires automatically via a post-deploy health check step. Clean canary metrics trigger a full rollout. A nightly cron job runs the full 600-case golden eval suite and publishes pass-rate trends to an internal dashboard; a drop below 95% pages the on-call engineer.

Docker tag in production means the same tag resolves to different images at different times. When Kubernetes restarts a pod due to a node failure, it may pull a newer or older image than the one that passed tests. Always tag images with an immutable identifier — the full git SHA — and reference that exact tag in deployment manifests.

# Wrong: tag drifts over time
docker build -t registry/agent-api:latest .
kubectl set image deployment/agent-api api=registry/agent-api:latest

# Right: tag is immutable and traceable to a specific commit
GIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
docker build -t registry/agent-api:$GIT_SHA .
kubectl set image deployment/agent-api api=registry/agent-api:$GIT_SHA
Running full LLM evals on every PR

Full evaluation suites that call real model APIs cost money and take time. Running 500 golden cases on every pull-request branch means a team of ten developers spending many dollars per hour on eval API calls for branches that may never merge. Tier evals deliberately: zero LLM calls in PR pipelines, a small smoke set on merge to main, and the full suite nightly.
Storing API keys as plain environment variables
Printing environment variables in CI logs — whether accidentally via

or indirectly through a verbose test framework — exposes secrets in job logs accessible to all repository contributors. Use GitHub Encrypted Secrets for sensitive keys, mask them explicitly if a framework might echo them, and prefer OIDC federation to eliminate long-lived keys entirely.

A common rationalisation is that a one-line prompt change does not need staging validation. LLM behaviour is highly sensitive to small prompt edits; a single word change can shift tone, alter refusal behaviour, or break a structured output format. All changes — regardless of apparent size — must travel through the full pipeline.

Teams that automate deployment often assume forward-only delivery. Every production deployment must have a tested rollback path — typically redeploying the previous SHA-tagged image — documented in a runbook and executable with one command. Verify the rollback procedure works in staging quarterly.
— tag every Docker image with the full 40-character git SHA. Never overwrite a tag that has been deployed to any environment.
Keep PR CI under ten minutes
— parallelise independent jobs, cache dependency installs keyed on the lock file hash, and reserve LLM calls for post-merge stages. Slow CI discourages frequent commits.
— require all CI checks to pass and at least one reviewer to approve before a PR can merge, even for repository administrators.
Separate workflow files for CI and CD
— CI triggers on every pull-request event; CD triggers only on push to main. Mixing them risks triggering expensive deployments from feature branches.
Use GitHub Environments for deployment gates
— GitHub Environments allow required reviewers, deployment protection rules, and environment-scoped secrets all in one configuration step.
— key the uv or pip cache on the lock file SHA. A cache hit reduces install time from 90 seconds to under 5.
— always verify the deployment actually started by calling
and asserting a 200 response before marking the pipeline green.
— upload eval results to S3 or as workflow artifacts so the staging eval compares against the last known-good baseline rather than a hardcoded threshold.
— link the rollback procedure from the pipeline README so any engineer can execute it under pressure without searching for the right command.

Pipeline duration directly affects developer velocity. A CI job that takes 20 minutes discourages the short feedback cycles that make CI valuable. Concrete targets: PR CI under 8 minutes; merge-to-main CI plus smoke eval under 15 minutes; staging deploy plus staging eval under 20 minutes. Total time from merge to production should be under 30 minutes for most agent services.
GitHub Actions jobs that have no

dependency run in parallel on separate runners at no extra cost (within concurrency limits). Split a single long sequential job — install, lint, typecheck, tests — into parallel jobs that each restore from cache: a
job running concurrently cuts wall-clock time roughly in half.

The dominant cost in most Python CI jobs is installing dependencies. Cache the virtual environment directory keyed on the lock file hash; a cache hit completes in under two seconds while a miss triggers a full install of 60 to 200 packages. Docker layer caching works similarly — structure the Dockerfile so the dependency installation layer (slow, changes rarely) comes before the application code layer (fast, changes on every commit), so Docker only re-runs the expensive layer when dependencies actually change.
As the golden eval suite grows toward thousands of cases, nightly runs may take hours. Strategies to keep them manageable: run eval cases in parallel using
to match runner CPU count; batch API calls with async concurrency capped below rate limits using
; and cache model responses for deterministic cases (identical system prompt and input) so re-running unchanged cases adds no API cost.
— tightly integrated with GitHub PRs and branch events; generous free tier for public repos; OIDC to major clouds is built in. Best default choice for projects hosted on GitHub.
— built into GitLab; pipeline YAML supports complex DAGs natively; strong self-hosted runner support for air-gapped environments.
— mature platform with good caching primitives and config as code; useful when a project spans multiple SCM providers.
— self-hosted; maximum flexibility and plugin ecosystem; high operational overhead. Worth considering only for air-gapped environments or highly custom integrations.
— multi-platform builds (amd64 and arm64) from a single pipeline step; important if deploying to AWS Graviton or Apple Silicon-based runners.
— free for public repos; no additional authentication setup needed in GitHub Actions.
— production-grade registries with lifecycle policies to auto-delete old image tags and reduce storage cost.
— fast open-source CVE scanner with a GitHub Actions integration; scans OS packages and Python dependencies in under 30 seconds.
— GitOps operator that watches a manifest repository and syncs Kubernetes clusters; adds strong auditability but increases setup complexity.
— PaaS with a simple deploy command:
handles rollout and health checks for services that do not need Kubernetes.
— managed container deployment with built-in blue/green and canary routing support on AWS infrastructure.

# pyproject.toml — tool configuration committed to the repository
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
select = ["E", "F", "I", "UP"]

[tool.pyright]
pythonVersion = "3.11"
typeCheckingMode = "basic"
include = ["src"]

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
addopts = "-q --tb=short"

[tool.uv]
dev-dependencies = [
    "ruff>=0.4",
    "pyright>=1.1",
    "pytest>=8",
    "pytest-asyncio>=0.23",
]
The table below compares CI/CD pipelines against alternative delivery approaches for an agentic AI service. 'Rollback speed' assumes infrastructure already exists and an incident has been identified.
SHA tag links commit to pipeline to deploy

CI pipelines need credentials: API keys for LLM providers, cloud credentials for registry pushes and deployments, and database passwords for integration tests. The security hierarchy from best to worst: OIDC federation (no long-lived secrets stored anywhere) → GitHub Encrypted Secrets (stored encrypted, never printed in logs) → repository environment variables (visible to all contributors) → hardcoded values (never acceptable).

Use OIDC to assume AWS IAM roles from GitHub Actions without storing AWS access keys. The role's trust policy restricts access to a specific repository and branch, limiting the blast radius of a compromised runner or a leaked token.

# GitHub Actions OIDC to AWS — no long-lived keys
- name: Configure AWS credentials
  uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
  with:
    role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/github-ci-ecr
    role-session-name: ci-${{ github.run_id }}
    aws-region: us-east-1
    # No access-key-id or secret-access-key needed — short-lived token via OIDC

Pin all third-party GitHub Actions to their full commit SHA rather than a mutable version tag. A compromised action at a mutable tag can exfiltrate secrets from every pipeline that uses it. Use Dependabot or Renovate to automatically open pull requests that update pinned SHAs as new releases are published.

For mature setups, sign container images with Sigstore Cosign and verify signatures at deployment time. This ensures only images built by your CI pipeline can be deployed, preventing an attacker who gains registry write access from injecting a malicious image into production.

Flaky tests — tests that sometimes pass and sometimes fail without any code change — destroy confidence in the CI signal and lead developers to re-run pipelines blindly. Common sources in agent projects: integration tests that depend on external APIs with rate limits, eval cases with non-deterministic model outputs at temperature > 0, and tests that depend on system time. Fixes: mock external APIs in integration tests with recorded fixtures; set
for all eval calls; quarantine known-flaky tests into a non-blocking job while investigating the root cause.
Every production deployment must have a rollback procedure that an on-call engineer can execute in under five minutes. For Kubernetes:

reverts to the previous ReplicaSet. For ECS, redeploy the previous task definition revision number. The rollback command must be documented in a runbook linked directly from the pipeline README and verified working in staging at least quarterly.

Think of a CI/CD pipeline as a factory assembly line with quality inspection stations. Raw material (a code change) enters at one end. At each station, an automated inspector checks for a different class of defect: the first station checks syntax, the second checks logic, the third checks behaviour against expected agent outputs. Only material that passes every station advances to the next. At the end of the line, the finished product (a container image) is stamped with a serial number (the git SHA), placed in a warehouse (the container registry), and shipped in stages — first to a test market (staging), then to a fraction of real customers (canary), then to everyone.

The key insight is that inspection is always cheaper earlier in the line. A defect caught by ruff in two seconds costs far less to fix than one caught by a production incident at 2 AM. CI/CD is not bureaucracy — it is the discipline of moving quality enforcement as close to the source of defects as possible, so that every working hour spent writing agent code is protected by automated safety nets.
CI automates lint, type checking, unit tests, and integration tests on every code change, catching defects before they reach the shared main branch.
CD builds an immutable Docker image tagged with the git SHA on every merge to main, then promotes that exact image through staging and production without rebuilding.
Agent services require tiered eval pipelines: zero LLM calls on pull-request branches, a small smoke eval on merge to main, a broader staging eval, and a full golden suite nightly.
Never deploy the :latest Docker tag; always reference the git SHA so every running environment is traceable to a specific commit and pipeline run.
Use OIDC federation to assume cloud roles from CI runners — this eliminates long-lived API keys and reduces the blast radius of a compromised pipeline job.
Keep PR CI under ten minutes by parallelising independent jobs, caching dependency installs keyed on the lock file hash, and reserving LLM API calls for post-merge stages.
A canary deployment strategy — routing 10% of production traffic to the new image first — limits blast radius when a regression slips through automated gates.
Every production deployment must have a one-command rollback procedure documented in a runbook linked from the pipeline; verify it works in staging quarterly.
